UAA Honors College ePortfolio Class of 2026

Marcus
Montoya

This ePortfolio traces my journey through the UAA Honors College. It started in a research lab and took me to conference stages, clinic exam rooms, and Indigenous communities across Alaska. What I kept learning along the way: the best science means nothing if the people it's meant to help don't feel seen. Every artifact here is a moment where I tried to close that gap.

Degree B.S. Biological Sciences, Pre-Med
GPA 3.96
Graduating May 2026
Goal Physician and advocate for the unseen

✦ 01 /Honors GER

Honors GER

General education requirement completed through the Honors College.

Methods of Inquiry, URS A121
Honors GER
Spring 2023 3 credits
powered by science
️ Reflection

Before this course, "research" meant one thing to me: the pandemic. I remember asking my mom how we were going to get through COVID-19, and she said researchers would find a vaccine. That stuck with me. URS A121 was the first time I got to see what research actually looks like from the inside. I learned about research careers, about methods, about the question underneath all of it: how do we know what we know? I went in with almost no idea where I was headed. I left with a direction. Everything I did in the lab afterward started here.

02 /Track Approved Academic Courses

Track Approved Academic Courses

Courses approved as part of my Honors track, spanning microbiology, virology, research seminars, and bioinformatics.

First independent research with Dr. Briggs, AK U-RISE (BIOL A498)
Academic course
Fall 2023 1 credit
Dr. Briggs lab ✦ Fall 2023
️ Reflection

My first real research experience was a lot of failure, and I mean that in the best way. Working with Dr. Briggs through AK U-RISE, I was looking at the connection between pesticide exposure and antibiotic resistance in E. coli and Shewanella. The cultures would get contaminated. The results would not make sense. The protocols would not work the first time, or the second. What I actually gained wasn't technique, it was the patience to sit with something not working and try again anyway. That ended up being the most useful thing I brought into every lab after this one.

TB and COVID genomic surveillance, Biomed U-RISE Bortz virology lab (BIOL 498)
Academic course
Spring 2025 1 credit
Bortz virology lab ✦ 2024-25
Genomic Surveillance of TB in Preserved COVID-19 Nasal Swabs from Alaska
Research poster, Biomed U-RISE Program Pioneering Innovation Award winner
️ Reflection

In the Bortz virology lab, I processed COVID-19 nasal swabs and used RT-PCR and gel electrophoresis to study respiratory co-infections and build toward genomic surveillance of Tuberculosis. Alaska has TB rates over five times the national average, mostly in rural and Alaska Native communities. I thought about that constantly while I was running samples. The molecular skills I built here will stick with me. But more than that, working in this lab kept pulling me back to the same question: what good is the research if the people it's supposed to help don't trust the person delivering it?

Learning to present: URS 188 and URS 388a-b
Academic course
Spring 2023 / Fall 2023 / Spring 2025 1+1+1 credits
️ Reflection

URS 388 is where I practiced my first poster presentation. I was deep in the Bortz lab at the time, and standing next to that poster while strangers walked up and asked me questions was genuinely terrifying. I knew the science. Talking about it to someone outside the lab was a different skill entirely. These seminars gave me the space to build that skill, and it paid off. I won the Pioneering Innovation Award at the spring 2025 Showcase. That meant a lot, not just as recognition but as real evidence that what I was learning in these courses was actually working.

From reads to scaffolds: BIOL 455 Bioinformatics
Academic course
Spring 2026 4 credits
BIOL 455 Bioinformatics ✦ sp26
️ Reflection

BIOL 455 was a required course for my major, not a research placement, but it ended up being one of the more genuinely useful things I took. Learning to work on the Beowulf cluster through the command line, run sequencing reads through a pipeline, use tools like BLAST and HMMER, and build phylogenetic trees gave me a skill set I know I will come back to. Bioinformatics is increasingly part of how medicine and public health actually operate, and having that foundation, even at the coursework level, feels like something I will be glad I have.

03 /Co-curricular and Extracurricular

Co-curricular and Extracurricular

Research programs, conferences, presentations, and certifications that shaped my Honors journey outside the classroom.

Entering research culture: Biomed U-RISE orientation and Burkhead lab
Co-curricular
Summer 2024 3 credits
Biomed U-RISE ✦ Summer 2024
️ Reflection

U-RISE orientation was the first time I felt like I actually belonged in a research environment. That summer I also worked in the Burkhead lab, learning to care for rats and doing observational work connected to Wilson's disease. It was completely different from the microbiology I had done in my first placement, and that was the point. Learning to be precise and responsible about the care of living animals added something to my training that bench work alone hadn't. This summer also gave me a cohort of people asking the same questions I was: about health, about community, about what kind of researchers we actually wanted to be.

Biomed U-RISE research training, Bortz lab
Co-curricular
Fall 2024 – Spring 2025 5 credits
Bortz lab team ✦ 2024-25
TB/COVID Genomic Surveillance Research Poster
Research poster, Biomed U-RISE Program
️ Reflection

Getting into Biomed U-RISE as one of four students that year felt like a real turning point. Over the course of the year in the Bortz lab, I got genuinely fluent in molecular diagnostics: PCR, gel electrophoresis, RNA and DNA extraction, flow-cell technology. The research itself was about something that mattered to the communities I grew up around. That combination, real skills applied to real problems affecting real people I know, is exactly what I want medicine to look like.

Warming waters, changing fish: NNA Navigating New Arctic REU
Co-curricular
Spring 2026 2 credits
NNA REU ✦ Warming Waters
Warming Waters, Changing Fish: The Impact of Water Temperature on Arctic Fish Size
Research poster, NNA REU Program, presented at Alaska Forum 2026
️ Reflection

Every research project before NNA asked me to find the answer. This one asked me to listen first. Working with Indigenous community voices through Project Jukebox, we put traditional knowledge about warming rivers and declining salmon alongside Western climate science, not above or below it, alongside. When an Athabascan elder describes ice rushes that no longer come, that is data. It carries weight a temperature sensor can't. I think about that in clinical settings too. Before I diagnose, I need to listen. The patient's story isn't background noise. It's the whole point.

Research Showcase and Pioneering Innovation Award
Co-curricular
Spring 2025 and Spring 2026 0.5 + 0.5 credits
UAA Showcase ✦ Pioneering Innovation Award
️ Reflection

Standing next to that poster while people walked up and asked questions was its own kind of nerve-wracking, even after all the practice in URS 388. Winning the Pioneering Innovation Award at the spring 2025 Showcase alongside my labmate Isabel Ulofoshio was genuinely meaningful. We had worked on that research together, and being recognized for it together felt right. Not just as recognition, but as confirmation that the communication piece was clicking. Talking about TB and COVID surveillance in Alaska to an audience that sometimes includes people from the communities that data is about carries real weight. The award reminded me that doing the science and actually communicating it are equally part of the job.

Alaska Forum on the Environment: NNA public presentation
Co-curricular
Spring 2026 0.5 credits
Alaska Forum ✦ Spring 2026
️ Reflection

The Alaska Forum was the first time I presented at a formal session with a mic and a podium, not a poster where people wander up whenever. That difference is real. I had to stand up, get a room's attention, and deliver findings about warming rivers and Indigenous subsistence practices to an audience of policymakers, tribal representatives, and community members. Not just scientists. After we finished, a Native American man in the audience raised his hand. He wasn't asking a question. He was sharing his own story, one that lined up with exactly what we had found, and telling us that our research reflected something true about his experience. That moment meant more to me than any grade or award. Someone whose community we were talking about looked at our work and said: yes, you got it right. That is what research is supposed to do. It is the standard I want to hold myself to in medicine too.

Circumpolar Health Conference, Halifax, fall 2025
Co-curricular
Fall 2025 0.5 credits
Circumpolar Health Conf. ✦ Halifax
️ Reflection

Halifax was my first academic conference, and I went because of Biomed U-RISE. I didn't present, but being in the room still mattered. Sitting in those sessions, listening to researchers and physicians and community advocates from across the Arctic, I kept thinking about how the health challenges I had been studying in Anchorage, TB disparities, climate-driven food insecurity, were not unique to Alaska. They show up in Greenland, northern Canada, Scandinavia, Siberia. Being a student from Anchorage in that room for the first time made the scale of the work feel real. It also made it feel possible.

Research ethics: CITI certifications
Co-curricular
Spring 2023 0.75 credits
️ Reflection

Three CITI certifications completed before I ever set foot in a research lab. It is the kind of training that is easy to rush through, but it gave me a solid grounding in research ethics, human subjects protections, and responsible conduct before I started working with real patient samples. A good foundation to have.

✦ 04 / My Story

My Honors Journey

How four years of research, clinical work, and community engagement shaped my path to medicine.

Chapter 01
The hug at Neuroversion
Working as a medical assistant with chronic pain patients showed me that the gap between clinical data and patient experience is the most important space in medicine.
Chapter 02
The lab and the community
Conducting genomic surveillance of TB and COVID-19 while working in underserved Alaskan communities made clear that scientific communication is itself a public health act.
Chapter 03
Listening differently
The NNA REU taught me to center community knowledge alongside Western science, integrating Indigenous perspectives on warming rivers and subsistence practices into original research.
️‍
Chapter 04
Representation in medicine
Navigating multiple identities across healthcare, research, and community spaces taught me that representation in medicine is not optional - it is what makes patients trust the room.
Chapter 05
The goal
To practice medicine that centers the whole patient: their biology, their community, their identity, and their right to feel understood by their provider.

Marcus Montoya UAA Honors College Class of 2026 Made with care in Anchorage, AK️
